The **Japan Cup** (ジャパンカップ, *Japan Kappu*; [JPN G-1](/wiki/List_of_horse_races#Japan "List of horse races")) is a [Group 1 horse race](/wiki/Group_races "Group races") in Japan, held annually on the last Sunday of November, post time of 15:40 at [Tokyo Racecourse](/wiki/Tokyo_Racecourse "Tokyo Racecourse") in [Fuchū, Tokyo](/wiki/Fuch%C5%AB,_Tokyo "Fuchū, Tokyo"). It is a [flat](/wiki/Flat_racing "Flat racing") race run over a distance of 2400 metres (about 1+1⁄2 miles, or 12 [furlongs](/wiki/Furlong "Furlong")) with a maximum of 18 horses.

First run in 1981, the Japan Cup was created by the [Japanese Racing Association](/wiki/Japan_Racing_Association "Japan Racing Association") (JRA) to give local horses the opportunity to compete against those of international calibre and to promote goodwill within the racing community worldwide. Similar to the [Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe](/wiki/Prix_de_l%27Arc_de_Triomphe "Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe"), the [Melbourne Cup](/wiki/Melbourne_Cup "Melbourne Cup") and the [Breeders' Cup](/wiki/Breeders%27_Cup "Breeders' Cup"), the Japan Cup extends invitations to top-performing horses aged 3 and above from around the world. The race is one of the world's richest, with a total prize purse of over one billion yen since 2023, and regularly attracts an audience of 100,000 people.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) It is the middle leg of the informal "Autumn [Triple Crown](/wiki/Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing "Triple Crown of Thoroughbred Racing")" along with the [Tennō Shō (Autumn)](/wiki/Tenn%C5%8D_Sh%C5%8D_(Autumn) "Tennō Shō (Autumn)") and the [Arima Kinen](/wiki/Arima_Kinen "Arima Kinen").

Despite a short history, the Japan Cup has established itself as an international contest with winners from all over the world, and is regularly ranked highly in the [International Federation of Horseracing Authorities](/wiki/International_Federation_of_Horseracing_Authorities "International Federation of Horseracing Authorities") (IFHA)'s "Top 100 Group 1 Races of the Year" compilation due to its high quality and depth of racers. In its early history, the race was dominated by foreign horses, with 8 of the first 10 winners coming from abroad. However, in recent years the number of overseas entries has significantly declined, and in the last twenty years only one horse from outside of Japan has won.

## Course

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japan_Cup&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Course")]

[![Aerial view of the Tokyo Racecourse taken in 2019, showing the outline of the course the Japan Cup takes place on.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Tokyo-Racecourse_aerial_2019.jpg/250px-Tokyo-Racecourse_aerial_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tokyo-Racecourse_aerial_2019.jpg)

Aerial view of the Tokyo Racecourse taken in 2019

The Japan Cup is held at the [Tokyo Racecourse](/wiki/Tokyo_Racecourse "Tokyo Racecourse"), in the western [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") suburb of [Fuchū](/wiki/Fuch%C5%AB,_Tokyo "Fuchū, Tokyo"). At 2,400 metres, the race is run counter-clockwise around the oval turf track, which uses a blend of [Noshiba grass](/wiki/Zoysia_japonica "Zoysia japonica") and Italian [ryegrass](/wiki/Lolium "Lolium").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The Cup uses Tokyo Racecourse's A-course configuration, placing the track's fence rail in its innermost position.[[4]](#cite_note-TokyoTrack-4) This means the circumference measures 2,083 m (1.29 mi; 10.35 furlongs) and the width varies from 31 to 41 m (102 to 135 ft), offering competitors ample room to manoeuvre and overtake in ways that are not always available in similar lengthed races.[[5]](#cite_note-2025Criteria-5)

The track has several undulations, varying in size and length, with a sharp rise of 1.2 m (4 ft) over 60 m (200 ft) halfway through being one of the more challenging obstacles. The [homestretch](/wiki/Glossary_of_North_American_horse_racing#homestretch_or_stretch "Glossary of North American horse racing") is one of the longest in Japanese racing at 525 m (1,722 ft), often leading to dramatic late surges. At 400 m (1,310 ft) to the post, there is a sharp 2 metres of elevation gain over the next 120 m (394 ft), requiring jockeys to conserve their horse's stamina to overcome this final hurdle. The final stretch is flat, allowing for racers to focus entirely on one last spurt of speed towards the post.[[4]](#cite_note-TokyoTrack-4)[[6]](#cite_note-G1-1984-6)

Due to its length and demanding finish, the race tends to favour strong [closers](/wiki/Glossary_of_North_American_horse_racing#closer "Glossary of North American horse racing") who can handle the uphill run and quickly accelerate in the final straight.[[7]](#cite_note-7) There is a long 325 m (1,066 ft) before the first corner.[[8]](#cite_note-8) While theoretically this minimises early positional advantages, analysis of the 2014 to 2024 races shows that horses starting near the inner fence are significantly more likely to win.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japan_Cup&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japan_Cup&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early years")]

#### The inaugural running

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japan_Cup&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: The inaugural running")]

The Japan Cup's creation was motivated by the JRA's desire to ensure the horses racing in Japan measured up to the quality of international horses.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) Prior to its creation, and even in the years after, options were limited in Japan for foreign horses. Most races only allowed Japanese horses to compete, leaving Japan's horses isolated from the outside world.[[a]](#cite_note-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15) As such, the Japan Cup offered an opportunity to determine how Japan compared to the rest of the world on their home turf. The idea of "creating strong horses that can compete on the world stage" had been proposed by the JRA since at least the 1970s.[[15]](#cite_note-JRA注目-16) However, early efforts to establish an international race during that decade failed, due to a lack of interest from overseas racing authorities and domestic concerns of foreign competition disrupting the [stud market](/wiki/Stud_farm "Stud farm").[[13]](#cite_note-MrJC1-13) By the start of the 1980s, the JRA's relations with the main racing authorities in other countries had developed enough for them to host their proposed race.[[16]](#cite_note-17)

[![Screenshot from Global Impact: The Rise Of The Japanese Thoroughbred showing the end result of the 1981 Japan Cup - the winner, Mairzy Doates, can be seen passing the post, comfortably ahead of the best performing Japanese horse Gold Spencer](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/4d/Finish_of_the_1981_Japan_Cup.png/250px-Finish_of_the_1981_Japan_Cup.png)](/wiki/File:Finish_of_the_1981_Japan_Cup.png)

The finish of the inaugural 1981 Japan Cup, with [Mairzy Doates](/wiki/Mairzy_Doates "Mairzy Doates") (furthest right, pink cap) passing the post one length ahead of [Frost King](/wiki/Frost_King "Frost King") (nearest the rail, white cap). The best performing Japanese horse, Gold Spencer (yellow cap), can be seen some distance behind Mairzy Doates and Frost King.[[b]](#cite_note-19)

The inaugural Japan Cup took place in 1981, scheduled for late November to target the traditional Western horseracing off-season, and avoiding competing with October's [Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe](/wiki/Prix_de_l%27Arc_de_Triomphe "Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe").[[18]](#cite_note-MrJC3-20) Invitations were sent to trainers in Japan, the United States, Canada and India.[[c]](#cite_note-21) A horse from Turkey, [Dersim](/wiki/Dersim_(horse) "Dersim (horse)"), was also invited, but was injured shortly after arriving in Japan and could not compete. The original line-up of international horses was at the time deemed nothing special.[[19]](#cite_note-HistoryOfTokyoRacecourse-22) The most prestigious of the group was [The Very One](/wiki/The_Very_One "The Very One"),[[19]](#cite_note-HistoryOfTokyoRacecourse-22) a veteran horse with many [graded stakes](/wiki/Graded_stakes_races "Graded stakes races") victories, but was now six and a half years old,[[20]](#cite_note-23) an advanced age for a top-level thoroughbred.[[d]](#cite_note-25) Conversely, in Japan the country's entered horses were viewed as an "all star cast", featuring the top three placing racers in the year's recent autumn [Tennō Shō](/wiki/Tenn%C5%8D_Sh%C5%8D_(Autumn) "Tennō Shō (Autumn)").[[19]](#cite_note-HistoryOfTokyoRacecourse-22)[[22]](#cite_note-26)

The race was won by American mare [Mairzy Doates](/wiki/Mairzy_Doates "Mairzy Doates"), ridden by [Cash Asmussen](/wiki/Cash_Asmussen "Cash Asmussen"), who finished a [length](/wiki/Horse_length "Horse length")[[e]](#cite_note-27) ahead of Canadian-trained [Frost King](/wiki/Frost_King "Frost King"). Japan's best performer, [Gold Spencer](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Spencer&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gold Spencer (page does not exist)"), placed fifth.[[23]](#cite_note-FirstRunning-28)[[24]](#cite_note-29) The disparity between the Japanese and foreign horses' performances came as a major surprise to the JRA officials and wider Japanese horseracing industry,[[19]](#cite_note-HistoryOfTokyoRacecourse-22)[[25]](#cite_note-30)[[26]](#cite_note-31) and the race is credited as a turning point in Japan's efforts to seriously compete at an international level in horse racing.[[19]](#cite_note-HistoryOfTokyoRacecourse-22)[[27]](#cite_note-32)[[28]](#cite_note-33) When writing about the first Cup's events, historian [Ryōji Motomura](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%9C%AC%E6%9D%91%E5%87%8C%E4%BA%8C "ja:本村凌二") noted the depth of the initial Japanese disappointment: "Those who watched that race must have sighed in deep regret, thinking that it would be another 20 years before a Japanese horse won the Japan Cup, something that would happen in the 21st century."[[29]](#cite_note-34)

#### Runnings in the 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japan_Cup&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Runnings in the 1980s")]

In the Cup's second year the range of invited countries was expanded to include participation from Europe and Oceania. However, the race was once again won by an American horse.[[30]](#cite_note-1982JC-35) [Half Iced](/wiki/Half_Iced "Half Iced"), the last international horse to enter the race,[[31]](#cite_note-36) narrowly defeated French-trained fillies [All Along](/wiki/All_Along "All Along") and [April Run](/wiki/April_Run "April Run"), with the Irish mare [Stanerra](/wiki/Stanerra "Stanerra") in fourth. Once again, the best performing Japanese horse, [Hikari Duel](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%92%E3%82%AB%E3%83%AA%E3%83%87%E3%83%A6%E3%83%BC%E3%83%AB "ja:ヒカリデユール"), placed fifth.[[30]](#cite_note-1982JC-35)

Stanerra returned the next year after a successful season in Europe, having recently won the Group 2 [Prince of Wales's Stakes](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales%27s_Stakes "Prince of Wales's Stakes") and Group 1[[f]](#cite_note-38) [Joe McGrath Memorial Stakes](/wiki/Joe_McGrath_Memorial_Stakes "Joe McGrath Memorial Stakes"), winning the Cup by a [head](/wiki/Horse_length#Other_measures "Horse length") over Japanese-trained [Kyoei Promise](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%82%AD%E3%83%A7%E3%82%A6%E3%82%A8%E3%82%A4%E3%83%97%E3%83%AD%E3%83%9F%E3%82%B9 "ja:キョウエイプロミス").[[33]](#cite_note-39) Stanerra's victory was highlighted by the [Liverpool Daily Post](/wiki/Liverpool_Daily_Post "Liverpool Daily Post") as an example of the growing trend in the 1980s of European mares matching the racing performance of stallions.[[34]](#cite_note-40) Her victory is also credited with strengthening [the Republic of Ireland's diplomatic relations with Japan](/wiki/Ireland%E2%80%93Japan_relations "Ireland–Japan relations"); since 1990, the [Irish Government](/wiki/Irish_Government "Irish Government") traditionally presents the Cup's winner with the Ambassador of Ireland Prize.[[35]](#cite_note-41)

1984 was the first year Japanese horse races received official [grading](/wiki/Group_races "Group races"), classifying the Cup as a national-level Group 1 race.[[g]](#cite_note-43)[[6]](#cite_note-G1-1984-6) The race itself was the first ever showdown between two Japanese [Triple Crown](/wiki/Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing#Japan "Triple Crown of Thoroughbred Racing") winners: [Mr. C. B.](/wiki/Mr._C._B. "Mr. C. B.") and the undefeated [Symboli Rudolf](/wiki/Symboli_Rudolf "Symboli Rudolf"). They had won their Crowns a year apart, nearly twenty years after [Shinzan](/wiki/Shinzan "Shinzan") became the last horse to do so in 1963, leading to much discussion by Japanese fans over which of the two horses were superior. There was an expectation that one of them would be the first Japanese horse to win the event in the "Triple Crown Showdown".[[37]](#cite_note-44) However, it was the lesser-fancied Japanese [Katsuragi Ace](/wiki/Katsuragi_Ace "Katsuragi Ace") who claimed victory, defeating British-trained [Bedtime](/w/index.php?title=Bedtime_(horse)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bedtime (horse) (page does not exist)") by 1+1⁄2 lengths, with Symboli Rudolf narrowly behind in third and Mr C. B. trailing in tenth.[[38]](#cite_note-45)[[39]](#cite_note-46) Japan achieved a second victory the next year, with Symboli Rudolf returning to triumph over Japanese-trained [Rocky Tiger](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%AD%E3%83%84%E3%82%AD%E3%83%BC%E3%82%BF%E3%82%A4%E3%82%AC%E3%83%BC "ja:ロツキータイガー").[[40]](#cite_note-y1985-47)

In 1986, [Jupiter Island](/wiki/Jupiter_Island_(horse) "Jupiter Island (horse)"), ridden by [Pat Eddery](/wiki/Pat_Eddery "Pat Eddery"), became the first British-trained horse to win the Cup, narrowly defeating fellow British runner [Allez Milord](/w/index.php?title=Allez_Milord&action=edit&redlink=1 "Allez Milord (page does not exist)").[[41]](#cite_note-48) The result was only confirmed after an inquiry by racecourse stewards following an objection by Allez Milord's rider [Greville Starkey](/wiki/Greville_Starkey "Greville Starkey"), who alleged the two horses had collided 60 metres from the post.[[42]](#cite_note-google5124-49) Jupiter Island's victory more than doubled the previous prize money the horse had accumulated in his previous forty starts, and at seven years old, he remains the oldest horse to win the Cup.[[43]](#cite_note-Timeform1986-50)[[44]](#cite_note-45thPressRelease-51)

[Le Glorieux](/wiki/Le_Glorieux "Le Glorieux"), trained in France by [Robert Collet](/wiki/Robert_Collet "Robert Collet") and ridden by [Alain Lequeux](/wiki/Alain_Lequeux "Alain Lequeux"), captured the 1987 edition,[[45]](#cite_note-52) while [Pay the Butler](/wiki/Pay_the_Butler "Pay the Butler"), trained by [Robert J. Frankel](/wiki/Robert_J._Frankel "Robert J. Frankel") and ridden by [Chris McCarron](/wiki/Chris_McCarron "Chris McCarron"), secured the United States' third victory the year after. Having previously only won a single major race, the Group 2 [Red Smith Handicap](/wiki/Red_Smith_Handicap "Red Smith Handicap") ran six months prior, Pay the Butler's win was regarded as a major upset over the year's Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe winner [Tony Bin](/wiki/Tony_Bin "Tony Bin") and Japanese favourites [Oguri Cap](/wiki/Oguri_Cap "Oguri Cap") and [Tamamo Cross](/wiki/Tamamo_Cross "Tamamo Cross").[[46]](#cite_note-53)[[47]](#cite_note-54)[[48]](#cite_note-55)[[49]](#cite_note-1988JC-56)

### A decade of global competition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japan_Cup&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: A decade of global competition")]

[![Gray horse with black mane standing in a grassy field.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Oguri_Cap_in_Yushun_Stallion_station.jpg/250px-Oguri_Cap_in_Yushun_Stallion_station.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oguri_Cap_in_Yushun_Stallion_station.jpg)

[Oguri Cap](/wiki/Oguri_Cap "Oguri Cap"), a rare triple entrant in the early days of the Japan Cup (1988—1990)

New Zealand mare [Horlicks](/wiki/Horlicks_(horse) "Horlicks (horse)") beat [Oguri Cap](/wiki/Oguri_Cap "Oguri Cap") by a [neck](/wiki/Horse_length#Other_measures "Horse length") in 1989, setting a new world record time for 2400 metres[[50]](#cite_note-1989Race-57)[[51]](#cite_note-58) and becoming Australia and New Zealand's highest stakes winner.[[52]](#cite_note-59) Her win marked the beginning of a decade of several countries vying for supremacy. [Better Loosen Up](/wiki/Better_Loosen_Up "Better Loosen Up") became the first Australian winner in 1990, prevailing in a close finish over France's Ode and Britain's [Cacoethes](/wiki/Cacoethes_(horse) "Cacoethes (horse)").[[53]](#cite_note-60) In 1991, the United States achieved its fourth victory in the Cup with [Golden Pheasant](/wiki/Golden_Pheasant_(horse) "Golden Pheasant (horse)"). The horse, a rare example of a thoroughbred jointly owned by two parties ([Los Angeles Kings](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Kings "Los Angeles Kings") [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") team owner [Bruce McNall](/wiki/Bruce_McNall "Bruce McNall") and [NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") ice hockey player [Wayne Gretzky](/wiki/Wayne_Gretzky "Wayne Gretzky")), has been described as the final major triumph for American-trained entrants in the Cup.[[54]](#cite_note-PheasantRetro-61)

Japanese-trained horses triumphed the next three years, although each time an overseas horse placed second. [Tokai Teio](/wiki/Tokai_Teio "Tokai Teio") emerged victorious in 1992,[[55]](#cite_note-62) followed by [Legacy World](/wiki/Legacy_World "Legacy World") in 1993[[56]](#cite_note-63) and [Marvelous Crown](/wiki/Marvelous_Crown "Marvelous Crown") in 1994.[[57]](#cite_note-64) The 1993 race also saw the only instance in the Cup's history of a fine being issued; the French horse [Kotashaan](/wiki/Kotashaan "Kotashaan")'s jockey, [Kent Desormeaux](/wiki/Kent_Desormeaux "Kent Desormeaux"), was fined $460 (equivalent to $1,000 in 2025) for easing his horse after mistaking the 100 meter mark for the finish.[[58]](#cite_note-1992Prize361Million-65)[[59]](#cite_note-66)

Despite bookmakers expecting a fourth straight Japanese win through [Hishi Amazon](/wiki/Hishi_Amazon "Hishi Amazon"),[[60]](#cite_note-67)[[61]](#cite_note-LandoStar-68) in 1995 the Cup was won for the first time by a German-trained horse, [Lando](/wiki/Lando_(horse) "Lando (horse)"). The race, which Lando's jockey [Michael Roberts](/wiki/Michael_Roberts_(jockey) "Michael Roberts (jockey)") considers his greatest memory,[[62]](#cite_note-69) was a significant milestone for German entrants; previously, the only German horse to place in the top five was Platini in 1993.[[61]](#cite_note-LandoStar-68) The race was also the first time no Japanese-born horse placed in the top five, with [Narita Brian](/wiki/Narita_Brian "Narita Brian") finishing sixth 1+3⁄4 lengths behind the American [Awad](/wiki/Awad_(horse) "Awad (horse)").[[63]](#cite_note-70)

British trainer [Michael Stoute](/wiki/Michael_Stoute "Michael Stoute") captured back-to-back victories, winning with the Irish horses [Singspiel](/wiki/Singspiel_(horse) "Singspiel (horse)") in 1996 and [Pilsudski](/wiki/Pilsudski_(horse) "Pilsudski (horse)") in 1997. Singspiel's nose-length victory made him Britain's leading prize money earner,[[64]](#cite_note-71)[[65]](#cite_note-JohnInglesSportingLife-72) while Pilsudski's win marked both his final career start and the fifth country in which he won a Group 1 race.[[66]](#cite_note-73) Singspiel's jockey, [Frankie Dettori](/wiki/Frankie_Dettori "Frankie Dettori"), was also originally scheduled to ride in 1997, on the British horse Mons. However, Dettori was replaced a week before by [Michael Kinane](/wiki/John_Reid_(jockey) "John Reid (jockey)") after receiving a riding ban.[[67]](#cite_note-:1-74) Dettori, who in his initial response stated his shock at having to miss the Japan Cup,[[67]](#cite_note-:1-74) had to wait until 2002 to enter the race again and secure his second Cup victory.

### Japanese dominance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japan_Cup&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Japanese dominance")]

[![El Condor Pasa, winner the 1998 Japan Cup, at his retirement ceremony in November 1999. Some of the racecourse's staff can be seen nearby helping navigate him.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/El_Condor_Pasa_19991128I1.jpg/250px-El_Condor_Pasa_19991128I1.jpg)](/wiki/File:El_Condor_Pasa_19991128I1.jpg)

[El Condor Pasa](/wiki/El_Condor_Pasa_(horse) "El Condor Pasa (horse)"), the winner of the 1998 Japan Cup

Between 1998 and 2004, the Japanese contingent's performance shifted noticeably. In 1998, [El Condor Pasa](/wiki/El_Condor_Pasa_(horse) "El Condor Pasa (horse)") led a historic sweep for Japan, finishing ahead of [Air Groove](/wiki/Air_Groove "Air Groove") and [Special Week](/wiki/Special_Week "Special Week") in the race's first-ever Japanese one-two-three.[[68]](#cite_note-75) Special Week went on to win the next year's race against a strong international field, including the year's [Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe](/wiki/Prix_de_l%27Arc_de_Triomphe "Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe") winner [Montjeu](/wiki/Montjeu "Montjeu") from Ireland. [Yutaka Take](/wiki/Yutaka_Take "Yutaka Take"), Special Week's jockey, remarked at the time it'd been a long-term career goal to win the event; Take has gone on to win the Cup several times over the next three decades.[[69]](#cite_note-76)[[70]](#cite_note-77)

In 2000, the Japanese horse [T. M. Opera O](/wiki/T._M._Opera_O "T. M. Opera O") entered the Japan Cup unbeaten for the season and maintained his year-long record, defeating his fellow Japanese rivals [Meisho Doto](/wiki/Meisho_Doto "Meisho Doto") and [Fantastic Light](/wiki/Fantastic_Light "Fantastic Light") by respectively, a neck and a nose behind in third. With this victory's prize money, T. M. Opera O became the highest-earning racehorse in world history at ¥1,216,477,000 (equivalent to ¥1,355,000,000 in 2024), overtaking the North American record held by [Cigar](/wiki/Cigar_(horse) "Cigar (horse)") and the previous Japanese record held by Special Week.[[71]](#cite_note-78) T. M. Opera O went on to win the year's [Arima Kinen](/wiki/Arima_Kinen "Arima Kinen"); having already won the year's autumn [Tennō Shō](/wiki/Tenn%C5%8D_Sh%C5%8D_(Autumn) "Tennō Shō (Autumn)"), he was awarded an additional ¥200,000,000 (equivalent to ¥223,000,000 in 2024) by the JRA for winning the three races, an accomplishment dubbed as completing the "Autumn Triple Crown". The same reward has been offered every year since to any horse that is able to win the trifecta of races.[[72]](#cite_note-AutumnTripleCrown-79)[[73]](#cite_note-OfficialBonus-80)

T. M. Opera O could not repeat his success the year after, losing to [Jungle Pocket](/wiki/Jungle_Pocket "Jungle Pocket") by a neck. For the first time Japanese-trained horses filled the top five positions. [Golan](/wiki/Golan_(horse) "Golan (horse)"), trained by Michael Stoute, was the best foreign finisher of the race in sixth.[[74]](#cite_note-81)

The Japan Cup was temporarily moved in 2002 to [Nakayama Racecourse](/wiki/Nakayama_Racecourse "Nakayama Racecourse") due to renovations at Tokyo Racecourse. The race was held on the right-handed outer loop course, switching the race's direction and reducing its distance to 2,200 m (1.37 mi; 10.94 furlongs).[[h]](#cite_note-83)[[76]](#cite_note-84) The year also brought Italy's greatest success in the event, with the Italian-trained [Falbrav](/wiki/Falbrav "Falbrav") securing a narrow victory over the American-trained [Sarafan](/w/index.php?title=Sarafan_(horse)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sarafan (horse) (page does not exist)"), with Japanese runner [Symboli Kris S](/wiki/Symboli_Kris_S "Symboli Kris S") finishing a close third.[[77]](#cite_note-85)

The Cup returned to Tokyo the next year, where [Tap Dance City](/wiki/Tap_Dance_City "Tap Dance City") went [wire-to-wire](/wiki/Wire-to-wire "Wire-to-wire") to achieve the largest winning margin ever recorded in the race's history at nine lengths.[[78]](#cite_note-86) The 2004 race saw [Zenno Rob Roy](/wiki/Zenno_Rob_Roy "Zenno Rob Roy") lead another Japanese sweep of the podium, against a series of lackluster performances from the international racers.[[79]](#cite_note-ZRR-87) Having won the year's autumn Tennō Shō, Zenno Rob Roy went on to triumph in the Arima Kinen the following month, becoming the second, and most recent, horse to achieve the Autumn Triple Crown.[[72]](#cite_note-AutumnTripleCrown-79)

### International win drought

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japan_Cup&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: International win drought")]

In 2005, British-trained [Alkaased](/wiki/Alkaased "Alkaased"), ridden by Frankie Dettori, won in a [photo finish](/wiki/Photo_finish "Photo finish") over the Japanese horse [Heart's Cry](/wiki/Heart%27s_Cry_(horse) "Heart's Cry (horse)"), with Zenno Rob Roy finishing third. The race set a new world record for the race's 2,400 metres at 2:22.1, surpassing the previous record set by Horlicks in 1989.[[80]](#cite_note-88)[[81]](#cite_note-89) Moreover, this was the final victory by a foreign horse in for nearly two decades.[[82]](#cite_note-:2-90)

In 2006, a few weeks after being disqualified from the year's Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe after testing positive for [ipratropium](/wiki/Ipratropium_bromide "Ipratropium bromide"),[[83]](#cite_note-91) [Deep Impact](/wiki/Deep_Impact_(horse) "Deep Impact (horse)") returned to racing in his home country at the Japan Cup and secured victory in his career's penultimate race.[[84]](#cite_note-92)[[85]](#cite_note-93) In part due to this victory, he was later named [Japanese Horse of the Year](/wiki/Japanese_Horse_of_the_Year "Japanese Horse of the Year") for the second consecutive year.[[86]](#cite_note-94) Similar circumstances occurred the year later, when [Admire Moon](/wiki/Admire_Moon "Admire Moon") won the Cup by a head over [Pop Rock](/wiki/Pop_Rock_(horse) "Pop Rock (horse)");[[87]](#cite_note-95) this was Admire Moon's final race before retiring and being crowned 2007's Japanese Horse of the Year.[[88]](#cite_note-96)[[89]](#cite_note-97)

[Screen Hero](/wiki/Screen_Hero "Screen Hero") was considered an outsider in the 2008 race, having competed in only four races that year following an 11-month hiatus due to a leg injury. Sent off at odds of 40/1, he defied expectations to win the race, finishing half a length ahead of [Deep Sky](/wiki/Deep_Sky_(horse) "Deep Sky (horse)").[[90]](#cite_note-98)

After finishing fourth in 2007 and third in 2008, [Vodka](/wiki/Vodka_(horse) "Vodka (horse)") finally claimed victory in her third attempt at the Japan Cup in 2009, winning in a photo finish over the 2008 [Kikuka-shō](/wiki/Kikuka-sh%C5%8D "Kikuka-shō") winner [Oken Bruce Lee](/wiki/Oken_Bruce_Lee "Oken Bruce Lee"). Vodka's final time of 2:22.4 was the third-fastest in the race's history, and was the first time a Japanese mare had won the event,[[91]](#cite_note-99) as well as making her the first mare worldwide to earn over $10 million (roughly ¥ 936,000,000) in prize money in their career.[[92]](#cite_note-100)

The 2010 race ended with a controversial disqualification.[[93]](#cite_note-VistaWin-101)[[94]](#cite_note-VistaWin3-102) [Buena Vista](/wiki/Buena_Vista_(horse) "Buena Vista (horse)"), the race favourite, finished first past the post two lengths ahead of [Rose Kingdom](/wiki/Rose_Kingdom "Rose Kingdom"). However, an inquiry relegated Buena Vista to second position, having been determined to have abruptly veered inward on the final straight and impeding Rose Kingdom's progress. This meant Rose Kingdom was declared the winner.[[95]](#cite_note-103)[[96]](#cite_note-VistaWin2-104) The disqualification was unpopular both within Japan and internationally due to Buena Vista's strong performance in the race, with the race's stewards being referred to as incompetent, resulting in the JRA revising its inquiry rules to meet international horse racing standards.[[96]](#cite_note-VistaWin2-104)[[97]](#cite_note-DonnaWin-105) Buena Vista returned in 2011 to claim the title outright.[[93]](#cite_note-VistaWin-101)[[94]](#cite_note-VistaWin3-102)

### Double victories and Triple Crown Showdowns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japan_Cup&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Double victories and Triple Crown Showdowns")]

#### Gentildonna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japan_Cup&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Gentildonna")]

[![Gentildonna crossing the finish line at the 2013 Japan Cup in first place, a nose ahead of the horse Denim and Ruby.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Gentildonna_Japan_Cup_2013%28IMG1%29.jpg/250px-Gentildonna_Japan_Cup_2013%28IMG1%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gentildonna_Japan_Cup_2013(IMG1).jpg)

[Gentildonna](/wiki/Gentildonna "Gentildonna") nosing out [Denim and Ruby](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%87%E3%83%8B%E3%83%A0%E3%82%A2%E3%83%B3%E3%83%89%E3%83%AB%E3%83%93%E3%83%BC "ja:デニムアンドルビー") at the 2013 Japan Cup, becoming the first double winner

In 2012, the final stretch of the race came down to [Orfevre](/wiki/Orfevre "Orfevre"), the 2011 Japanese Triple Crown winner, facing off against the 2012 Fillies' Triple Crown[[i]](#cite_note-106) winner [Gentildonna](/wiki/Gentildonna "Gentildonna"). In a closely contested ending, Gentildonna claimed victory by a nose. However, a stewards' inquiry afterwards confirmed that Gentildonna made contact with Orfervre in the final stretch. The result stood, but Gentildonna's jockey, [Yasunari Iwata](/wiki/Yasunari_Iwata "Yasunari Iwata"), was suspended for two days.[[97]](#cite_note-DonnaWin-105)[[98]](#cite_note-107) Gentildonna returned the next year to win the Cup again, becoming the first horse to win the race twice.[[99]](#cite_note-108) She attempted a third consecutive victory in 2014 in her final race, but came fourth whilst [Epiphaneia](/wiki/Epiphaneia_(horse) "Epiphaneia (horse)"), who had entered at the relatively low odds of 15–1, claimed victory.[[100]](#cite_note-109)

The 2014 race was the first race in the event's history to be sponsored,[[101]](#cite_note-110) after the JRA began searching for suitable sponsors the previous year following a gradual decade-long decline in turnover.[[102]](#cite_note-111) 2014 also saw the first release of the [International Federation of Horseracing Authorities](/wiki/International_Federation_of_Horseracing_Authorities "International Federation of Horseracing Authorities")' "Top 100 Group 1 Races of the Year for 3yo's and upwards", a report which ranks Group 1 races by the average IFHA ratings of their top finishers over the previous three runnings.[[103]](#cite_note-112) The Japan Cup placed eighth in the inaugural report,[[104]](#cite_note-113) and since then has regularly placed in the top ten each year, including first in 2023.[[105]](#cite_note-114)[[106]](#cite_note-115)[[107]](#cite_note-116)

#### Almond Eye

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japan_Cup&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Almond Eye")]

The Japanese filly [Shonan Pandora](/wiki/Shonan_Pandora "Shonan Pandora") captured the 2015 race, becoming the seventh mare to win the race.[[108]](#cite_note-117) [Kitasan Black](/wiki/Kitasan_Black "Kitasan Black") went wire-to-wire to comfortably win the year after, with fellow Japanese racers [Sounds of Earth](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%82%B5%E3%82%A6%E3%83%B3%E3%82%BA%E3%82%AA%E3%83%96%E3%82%A2%E3%83%BC%E3%82%B9 "ja:サウンズオブアース") and [Cheval Grand](/wiki/Cheval_Grand "Cheval Grand") finishing second and third respectively 2+1⁄2 lengths behind Kitasan Black.[[109]](#cite_note-118) Cheval Grand returned next year to claim victory, with the win securing the top position in the 2016 Longines World's Best Jockey Rankings for his jockey [Hugh Bowman](/wiki/Hugh_Bowman "Hugh Bowman").[[110]](#cite_note-119)

In 2018 Alkaased's course record was broken, when [Almond Eye](/wiki/Almond_Eye "Almond Eye") clocked 2:20.6 on firm ground. The race was another all-Japanese sweep, with Almond Eye finishing 1+3⁄4 lengths ahead of [Kiseki](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%82%AD%E3%82%BB%E3%82%AD_(%E7%AB%B6%E8%B5%B0%E9%A6%AC) "ja:キセキ (競走馬)"), with [Suave Richard](/wiki/Suave_Richard "Suave Richard") in third.[[111]](#cite_note-120) Her performance received great praise from commentators, including her being called one of the greatest horses in the world.[[112]](#cite_note-121)[[113]](#cite_note-122)

[![Suave Richard crossing the finish line at the 2019 Japan Cup, winning the race, 3/4ths of a length ahead of Curren Bouquetd'or, who can also be seen in the picture.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Suave_Richard_Japan_Cup_2019%28IMG1%29.jpg/250px-Suave_Richard_Japan_Cup_2019%28IMG1%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Suave_Richard_Japan_Cup_2019(IMG1).jpg)

[Suave Richard](/wiki/Suave_Richard "Suave Richard") crossing the finish line in first place at the 2019 Japan Cup

2019's Japan Cup was the first in which all the entrants were domestic racers, with no international horses signing up for the event. It was also a memorial race for the late Deep Impact, who had been [euthanised](/wiki/Animal_euthanasia "Animal euthanasia") in July earlier that year. Several runners, including runner-up [Curren Bouquetd'or](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%82%AB%E3%83%AC%E3%83%B3%E3%83%96%E3%83%BC%E3%82%B1%E3%83%89%E3%83%BC%E3%83%AB "ja:カレンブーケドール") and third-placed [Wagnerian](/wiki/Wagnerian_(horse) "Wagnerian (horse)"), were progeny of Deep Impact.[[114]](#cite_note-123) [Suave Richard](/wiki/Suave_Richard "Suave Richard"), who was sired by [Heart's Cry](/wiki/Heart%27s_Cry_(horse) "Heart's Cry (horse)"), delivered a strong late run to win under the year's [British Champion Jockey](/wiki/British_flat_racing_Champion_Jockey "British flat racing Champion Jockey"), [Oisin Murphy](/wiki/Oisin_Murphy "Oisin Murphy").[[115]](#cite_note-124)[[116]](#cite_note-125)

The 2020 Japan Cup had a significant amount of excitement prior to the event due to a very strong line-up, in particular featuring three Japanese Triple Crown winners; the 2018 Fillies Triple Crown winner Almond Eye, the undefeated 2020 Triple Crown winner [Contrail](/wiki/Contrail_(horse) "Contrail (horse)"), and the undefeated 2020 Fillies Triple Crown winner [Daring Tact](/wiki/Daring_Tact "Daring Tact").[[117]](#cite_note-126)[[118]](#cite_note-127)[[119]](#cite_note-128) These three horses all placed in the top three, with Almond Eye triumphing in the Cup again by 1+1⁄4 lengths over Contrail and Daring Tact.[[120]](#cite_note-129)[[121]](#cite_note-130) Due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), 2020 recorded the lowest crowd attendance in the race's history by a significant margin at 4,604 people,[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) following the JRA's decision to hold all of their races that year behind closed doors.[[122]](#cite_note-131)

### Recent races

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japan_Cup&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Recent races")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/20231126%E3%82%B8%E3%83%A3%E3%83%91%E3%83%B3%E3%82%AB%E3%83%83%E3%83%97.jpg/250px-20231126%E3%82%B8%E3%83%A3%E3%83%91%E3%83%B3%E3%82%AB%E3%83%83%E3%83%97.jpg)](/wiki/File:20231126%E3%82%B8%E3%83%A3%E3%83%91%E3%83%B3%E3%82%AB%E3%83%83%E3%83%97.jpg)

Equinox at the 2023 Japan Cup

Contrail avenged his loss from the previous year by winning the 2021 Cup in his final race before retirement, finishing two lengths ahead of the Japanese Authority.[[123]](#cite_note-132) Despite racing only three times previously in the year, his prior performance meant Contrail had been the odds-on favourite,[[124]](#cite_note-133) and his victory at the Cup contributed to him securing the 2021 JRA award for Best Older Colt or Horse.[[125]](#cite_note-134)

[Vela Azul](/wiki/Vela_Azul "Vela Azul") was the victor in 2022, his first Group 1 title.[[126]](#cite_note-135) The horse had spent the majority of his previous races (16 out of 21) on dirt tracks, only switching to turf at the Awaji Tokubetsu [allowance race](/wiki/Glossary_of_North_American_horse_racing#allowance_race "Glossary of North American horse racing") in March 2022, five races before the Japan Cup.[[127]](#cite_note-136)[[128]](#cite_note-137) The race favourite, [Shahryar](/wiki/Shahryar_(horse) "Shahryar (horse)"), placed second, and in the closing moments of the race was involved in an incident where he drifted left in the closing stages, significantly impeding [Danon Beluga's](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%80%E3%83%8E%E3%83%B3%E3%83%99%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%82%AC "ja:ダノンベルーガ") progress. The JRA stewards judged this careless riding and banned Shahryar's rider, [Cristian Demuro](/wiki/Cristian_Demuro "Cristian Demuro"), from racing for four days.[[129]](#cite_note-138)

2023 was another year with a line-up considered especially strong, featuring multiple Group 1 winners such as the year's [Saudi Cup](/wiki/Saudi_Cup "Saudi Cup")'s winner [Panthalassa](/wiki/Panthalassa_(horse) "Panthalassa (horse)") and the seventh [Japanese Triple Tiara](/wiki/Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing#Japanese_Triple_Tiara "Triple Crown of Thoroughbred Racing") winner [Liberty Island](/wiki/Liberty_Island_(horse) "Liberty Island (horse)").[[130]](#cite_note-139) In spite of the quality of the competition [Equinox](/wiki/Equinox_(horse) "Equinox (horse)"), the world's highest rated horse[[131]](#cite_note-140) and who was on a streak of five G1 wins, won in a victory widely described by reporters and the race's commentators as a commanding win.[[132]](#cite_note-141)[[133]](#cite_note-142)[[134]](#cite_note-143) The strength of Equinox's performance, combined with the field's depth, led to two honours: Equinox was named the year's World's Best Racehorse by the IFHA, and the Japan Cup the IFHA's World's Best Horse Race.[[135]](#cite_note-144)

Running near the back for much of the race, [Do Deuce](/wiki/Do_Deuce "Do Deuce") surged forward in the final stretch to win the 2024 Cup in his penultimate race, resulting in him being awarded both the year's JRA Japanese Horse of the Year and the JRA Award for Best Older Colt or Horse awards .[[136]](#cite_note-145) It also secured jockey Yutaka Take's record fifth win in the event's history.[[137]](#cite_note-146)

#### The end of the Japanese streak

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japan_Cup&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: The end of the Japanese streak")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/%E3%82%BF%E3%83%BC%E3%83%95%E3%82%A3%E3%83%BC%E3%83%93%E3%82%B8%E3%83%A7%E3%83%B3%E3%81%AB%E8%A8%98%E3%81%95%E3%82%8C%E3%81%9F%E3%83%AC%E3%82%B3%E3%83%BC%E3%83%89%E8%A8%98%E9%8C%B2%E3%81%A8%E3%82%AB%E3%83%A9%E3%83%B3%E3%83%80%E3%82%AC%E3%83%B3.jpg/250px-%E3%82%BF%E3%83%BC%E3%83%95%E3%82%A3%E3%83%BC%E3%83%93%E3%82%B8%E3%83%A7%E3%83%B3%E3%81%AB%E8%A8%98%E3%81%95%E3%82%8C%E3%81%9F%E3%83%AC%E3%82%B3%E3%83%BC%E3%83%89%E8%A8%98%E9%8C%B2%E3%81%A8%E3%82%AB%E3%83%A9%E3%83%B3%E3%83%80%E3%82%AC%E3%83%B3.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E3%82%BF%E3%83%BC%E3%83%95%E3%82%A3%E3%83%BC%E3%83%93%E3%82%B8%E3%83%A7%E3%83%B3%E3%81%AB%E8%A8%98%E3%81%95%E3%82%8C%E3%81%9F%E3%83%AC%E3%82%B3%E3%83%BC%E3%83%89%E8%A8%98%E9%8C%B2%E3%81%A8%E3%82%AB%E3%83%A9%E3%83%B3%E3%83%80%E3%82%AC%E3%83%B3.jpg)

Calandagan following his winning of the 2026 Japan Cup

The French-trained [Calandagan](/wiki/Calandagan "Calandagan") was the sole entrant from outside of Japan in 2025. However, in the lead-up to the event he was considered one of the best overseas participants in recent years,[[138]](#cite_note-147) having previously won three Group 1 races in the year, being ranked at the top of the Longines World's Best Racehorses Rankings, and had recently been declared the [Cartier Horse of the Year](/wiki/Cartier_Horse_of_the_Year "Cartier Horse of the Year").[[139]](#cite_note-148) Calandagan lived up to his strong reputation, surging in the last 300 metres to beat [Masquerade Ball](/wiki/Masquerade_Ball_(horse) "Masquerade Ball (horse)") by a head, breaking Almond Eye's world record for 2,400m on turf and marking the end of Japan's winning streak of 19 years.[[140]](#cite_note-149)[[141]](#cite_note-CalRecord-150) The race itself also drew attention from fans and reporters for featuring three jockeys getting thrown off their horses; [Yuga Kawada](/wiki/Yuga_Kawada "Yuga Kawada") who fell off [Admire Terra](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%82%A2%E3%83%89%E3%83%9E%E3%82%A4%E3%83%A4%E3%83%86%E3%83%A9 "ja:アドマイヤテラ") at the start of the race, and later [Christophe Lemaire](/wiki/Christophe_Lemaire "Christophe Lemaire") on Masquerade Ball and [Keita Tosaki](/wiki/Keita_Tosaki "Keita Tosaki") on [Danon Decile](/wiki/Danon_Decile "Danon Decile"), who collided after Masquerade Ball tried to avoid the jockeyless Admire Terra.[[82]](#cite_note-:2-90)[[142]](#cite_note-151)[[143]](#cite_note-152)

## Decline in international participation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japan_Cup&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Decline in international participation")]

[![A graph showing the number of foreign horses participating in the Japan Cup from 1981 to 2024. While the number of participants is high for the first ten years, a sharp decline after the 2000s can be seen, with the overall line of best fit going from ~68% in 1981 to ~10% in 2024 (True numbers; 47% to 21%).](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Participation_of_Foreign_Horses_in_the_Japan_Cup_%281981_-_2024%29.png/330px-Participation_of_Foreign_Horses_in_the_Japan_Cup_%281981_-_2024%29.png)](/wiki/File:Participation_of_Foreign_Horses_in_the_Japan_Cup_(1981_-_2024).png)

Participation of foreign horses in the Japan Cup (1981—2024), by total number and %

Between the American horse [Alkaased's](/wiki/Alkaased "Alkaased") and the Irish horse [Calandagan's](/wiki/Calandagan "Calandagan") victories in 2005 and 2025, only one non-Japanese horse has placed in the top three; the British [Ouija Board](/wiki/Ouija_Board_(horse) "Ouija Board (horse)"), who was third in 2006. All other top-three placers in this twenty-year period were born and trained in Japan, except for the 2024 runner-up [Shin Emperor](/wiki/Shin_Emperor "Shin Emperor"), who was born in France and brought over to Japan as a [foal](/wiki/Foal "Foal").[[144]](#cite_note-153) The number of international horses has also declined; while over half of the horses in the inaugural race were from abroad, by 2024 there were only three.[[145]](#cite_note-154) In more extreme cases, the number was even lower; [Iresine](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AC%E3%82%B8%E3%83%B3 "ja:イレジン"), a French horse, and Calandagan were the only entries from abroad in 2023 and 2025 respectively, and no foreign horses competed in 2019. This has led to commentators raising concerns about the Japan Cup maintaining its prestige or has "lost its way".[[146]](#cite_note-ESRJCTP-155)[[147]](#cite_note-NetKeibaForeignHorses-156) However, it has also been argued that the Cup's homogeneous winners since the 2000s is nothing unique and is instead an example of the home-advantage trends seen in other international races.[[148]](#cite_note-157)

The decline in international interest is often attributed to the rise in the overall strength of Japanese horses since the early 2000s.[[146]](#cite_note-ESRJCTP-155)[[149]](#cite_note-158)[[150]](#cite_note-159) The most commonly cited reason of this trend is the great success of leading sires [Sunday Silence](/wiki/Sunday_Silence "Sunday Silence") and [Northern Taste](/wiki/Northern_Taste "Northern Taste"), whose progeny from the 1990s onwards have consistently performed at a high level and produced numerous Group 1 winners.[[151]](#cite_note-160)[[152]](#cite_note-161) Additionally, the JRA has also encouraged trainers to breed their horses with international bloodlines to enhance their competitive edge,[[153]](#cite_note-162)[[154]](#cite_note-163) and has built facilities such as the [Bloodhorse Training Center](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E8%BB%BD%E7%A8%AE%E9%A6%AC%E8%82%B2%E6%88%90%E8%AA%BF%E6%95%99%E3%82%BB%E3%83%B3%E3%82%BF%E3%83%BC "ja:軽種馬育成調教センター") to facilitate further training and research.[[155]](#cite_note-164)[[156]](#cite_note-165)

The increasingly busy international racing calendar is a contributing factor to declining international numbers. In the Japan Cup's first years, November was "a time when once a comfortable silence fell over flat racing".[[43]](#cite_note-Timeform1986-50) However, since 1981, numerous high-profile worldwide competitions like the [Breeders' Cup](/wiki/Breeders%27_Cup "Breeders' Cup") and the [Hong Kong Vase](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Vase "Hong Kong Vase") have been established in the winter months. With similar levels of prestige and prize money, these races have given trainers a wider range of available international options,[[65]](#cite_note-JohnInglesSportingLife-72)[[157]](#cite_note-UmaToku-166) leading to suggestions that the Cup be moved to a less competitive time of the year.[[147]](#cite_note-NetKeibaForeignHorses-156)

Concerns have also been raised about the logistical challenges involved with bringing foreign horses to Japan. The process, which involves lengthy amounts of travel and [quarantine](/wiki/Quarantine "Quarantine"), can distress the horses involved, further making the idea of racing in Japan unappealing to foreign horse owners.[[158]](#cite_note-167) In 2022, the JRA opened a new quarantine stable near Tokyo Racecourse to help make travel to Japan for the Cup more enticing for trainers.[[159]](#cite_note-168)[[160]](#cite_note-169) The issues surrounding horses traveling from abroad have been noted as a problem since the early years of the Japan Cup. The 1983 winner [Stanerra](/wiki/Stanerra "Stanerra") arrived in Tokyo in bad shape from the flight and required six hours of walking daily before her race to rebuild her strength,[[161]](#cite_note-170) while the 1989 winner [Horlicks](/wiki/Horlicks_(horse) "Horlicks (horse)") required the use of a [full-length mirror](/wiki/Full-length_mirror "Full-length mirror") mired with other horses' scent to help overcome her stress and loneliness after arriving in Japan.[[162]](#cite_note-171)

## Purse and qualification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japan_Cup&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Purse and qualification")]

Throughout its history, the Japan Cup has been repeatedly described as one of the world's richest races.[[j]](#cite_note-175) In the original 1981 race, the prize pool totaled over ¥123,000,000 (equivalent to ¥174,000,000 in 2024), with ¥65,000,000 (equivalent to ¥91,800,000 in 2024) being awarded to the winner [Mairzy Doates](/wiki/Mairzy_Doates "Mairzy Doates").[[23]](#cite_note-FirstRunning-28) The purse's size increased steadily over the years, with the prize pool nearly ¥250,000,000 (roughly US$3.61 million[[58]](#cite_note-1992Prize361Million-65)) by 1992.[[166]](#cite_note-176) By 2023, the prize pool had reached over a billion yen.[[167]](#cite_note-177)

While the list of qualifying races varies slightly each year, typically foreign horses that win notable [Group 1](/wiki/Group_races "Group races") races outside of Japan will be invited to participate, with the incentive of an additional prize if they place well.[[168]](#cite_note-178) Japanese horses undergo standard JRA selection based on previous performances and their total career earnings.[[169]](#cite_note-179)[[170]](#cite_note-180) Horses that participate in the race and have also achieved victory in other specific JRA races in the year will also be eligible for bonuses.[[171]](#cite_note-181) For example, in 2025, the criteria were as follows:

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japan_Cup&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-14)** Japanese horses had on rare occasions competed abroad before 1981. However, outside of [Hakuchikara's](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%8F%E3%82%AF%E3%83%81%E3%82%AB%E3%83%A9 "ja:ハクチカラ") victory at the 1959 [Washington's Birthday Handicap](/wiki/San_Luis_Obispo_Handicap "San Luis Obispo Handicap"), their results were "a series of humiliating defeats", leading trainers at the time to view taking their horses outside Japan as a futile endeavour.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-MrJC1-13)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-19)** Screenshot is from the [Sky Racing](/wiki/Sky_Racing "Sky Racing") documentary, *Global Impact: The Rise Of The Japanese Thoroughbred.*[[17]](#cite_note-SkyDoc-18)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-21)** Although Britain and France had been considered important countries to include during the race's initial development, the JRA decided against extending invitations to them due to limited ties with their respective racing authorities. This decision was heavily criticised by Japanese media in the build-up to the race.[[18]](#cite_note-MrJC3-20)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-25)** A thoroughbred horse's peak performance typically occurs between 4 and 5 years old.[[21]](#cite_note-24)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-27)** A common horseracing measure in race results that represents the average length of a horse, around 8 ft (2.4 m).
6. **[^](#cite_ref-38)** An international grading standard regulated by the IFHA that denotes a race as having major international importance and where the partaking racers are expected to be of a very high standard.[[32]](#cite_note-37)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-43)** Upgraded to international Group 1 status in 1992.[[36]](#cite_note-42)
